topic: A person finds out their partner has been learning their native language
raw_output:
[story 1]

The silence in the apartment was thick, suffocating. I kept glancing at Mark, who was hunched over his laptop in the corner, a faint, rhythmic clicking sound emanating from him. It started subtly—a misplaced word here, a grammatical structure slightly off—but then I overheard him attempting a full conversation in my mother tongue. My stomach clenched, a tight knot forming just beneath my ribs. I watched his brow furrow, the concentration etched deep into his features, and a wave of anxiety washed over me. Every small sound he made, every syllable he forced out, felt like a tiny hammer blow against my composure. I wanted to ask him to stop, to just be normal, but the words caught in my throat, lodging there like shards of glass. I retreated to the kitchen, gripping the edge of the counter until my knuckles turned white, needing a physical anchor against the rising tide of unease.

[story 2]

I walked into the living room, the sound of his voice—a melodic, unfamiliar cadence—hitting me like a physical jolt. It was my language, the one I spoke with my grandmother, and he was speaking it fluently. My breath hitched. I stopped dead in the doorway, my hands instinctively flying up to cover my mouth, muffling a sharp intake of air. He looked up, a small, triumphant smile playing on his lips, oblivious to the sudden, seismic shift in the atmosphere. The air suddenly felt too thin to draw in. I felt a prickling sensation behind my eyes, a desperate urge to flee the room, to dismantle the situation entirely. I managed a strained, "Mark, can we talk for a minute?" My voice came out thin and reedy, a stark contrast to the rich sounds he was producing.

[story 3]

The afternoon light slanted across the floor, illuminating dust motes dancing in the air, but I couldn't focus on the light. My gaze kept snagging on Mark’s mouth as he practiced a phrase, the unfamiliar sounds rolling off his tongue with an unsettling ease. A tremor started in my hands; I found myself pacing the length of the rug, my movements jerky and agitated. Each footfall echoed too loudly in the quiet space, amplifying the internal churning. I wanted to turn around, to escape the visual confirmation of what was happening, but I remained rooted, watching him. A deep, hollow ache settled in my chest, a heavy weight pressing down, making it difficult to draw a full, steady breath. I swallowed hard, the dryness in my throat making the effort monumental.
